# Create a Workflow with Delay in MagicBell

## Background

MagicBell allows you to orchestrate multi-step notification campaigns using workflows. In this task, you will create a Node.js script that uses the MagicBell Node.js SDK to define and save a workflow containing a broadcast step, a wait (delay) step, and a follow-up broadcast step.

## Requirements

* Write a Node.js script that uses the MagicBell Node.js SDK (`magicbell-js/project-client`) to save a workflow definition.
* The workflow key must be `workflow-delay-${run-id}` where `run-id` is read from the `ZEALT_RUN_ID` environment variable.
* The workflow must consist of exactly three steps in this order:
  1. `broadcast` command: Send a notification with the title "Welcome!".
  2. `wait` command: Pause the workflow execution for 60 seconds.
  3. `broadcast` command: Send a follow-up notification with the title "Follow up".

## Implementation Hints

* Read the `run-id` from the `ZEALT_RUN_ID` environment variable.
* Initialize the `magicbell-js` `ProjectClient` using the `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN` environment variable.
* Use the `workflows.saveWorkflow` method to create the workflow definition.
* A `wait` step requires the `duration` input parameter to be specified in seconds.
* Ensure your script waits for the API call to complete before exiting.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/magicbell-task
* Command: node create-workflow\.js
* Input argument format: None (reads from environment variables).
* Expected command output format: The stdout should print a success message indicating the workflow was created.

Let me break down the task:

1. Create a Node.js script in `/home/user/magicbell-task/` that uses the MagicBell Node.js SDK
2. The script should create a workflow with:
   - A broadcast step with title "Welcome!"
   - A wait step with 60 seconds duration
   - A broadcast step with title "Follow up"
3. The workflow key must be `workflow-delay-${run-id}` where `run-id` comes from `ZEALT_RUN_ID` env var
4. Initialize ProjectClient using `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN` env var
5. Use `workflows.saveWorkflow` method

Let me first explore the MagicBell SDK to understand the API, then createthe script.

todos.content: Explore MagicBell SDK and project structure
Create the Node.js script for workflow creation

content.text:
Title: GitHub - magicbell/magicbell-js: TypeScript SDK monorepo for MagicBell - The real-time notification inbox for web & mobile apps

URL Source: https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js

Markdown Content:
This monorepo contains JavaScript/TypeScript based SDKs to build a notification inbox for your site, powered by [MagicBell](https://magicbell.com/).

## Documentation

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#documentation)
You can find the MagicBell documentation on [magicbell.com/docs](https://magicbell.com/docs). Documentation found in this README applies to the dev setup of this monorepo. It does not cover or apply to the consumption of the SDKs.

For SDK documentation see [magicbell.com/docs](https://magicbell.com/docs), or the package readmes at: [packages/core](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js/blob/main/packages/core), [packages/embeddable](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js/blob/main/packages/embeddable), [packages/magicbell-react](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js/blob/main/packages/react), [packages/react-headless](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js/blob/main/packages/react-headless).

## Requirements

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#requirements)
The development environment for this repo supports `node:v18` and up. We use `yarn` as package manager.

You'll need TypeScript 4.5 or higher for our bundled type definitions.

## Setup

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#setup)
Run the following commands in your terminal to get a copy of this repo, and install required dependencies.

```
git clone git@github.com:magicbell/magicbell-js.git
cd magicbell-js
yarn
```

### Packages

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#packages)
When working on the packages, you'll want to run the builder in watch mode. You can do so by:

```
yarn start
```

This builds the packages, and recompiles all affected ones when you make changes in the `packages` folder.

### Storybook

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#storybook)
We have a single storybook instance available for stories in all packages. Run it with:

```
yarn start:storybook
```

After that, visit [http://localhost:6006](http://localhost:6006/). Storybook will hot-reload on any change that you make in `/packages`. Make sure that you're running `yarn start` in another terminal, when you have the feeling that you're dealing with stale data.

### Example

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#example)
There is a vite based example application located in the /example/floating-inbox folder. This gives one the opportunity to try out use cases in a production like environment. Run it with:

```
yarn start:example
```

After that, visit [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000/). The example app will hot-reload on any change that you make in `/packages`. Make sure that you're running `yarn start` in another terminal, when you have the feeling that you're dealing with stale data.

The /example folder contains more examples like a Next.js Web Push app as well.

## Contribute

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#contribute)
Code quality is set up for you with `eslint`, `prettier`, `husky`, and `lint-staged`. Please keep the pre-commit hooks enabled.

## Optimizations

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#optimizations)
You can take advantage of [invariant](https://npmjs.com/tiny-invariant), [warning](https://npmjs.com/tiny-warning) and `__DEV__` to add development-only warnings which won't end up in our production bundles.

import invariant from 'tiny-invariant';
import warning from 'tiny-warning';

invariant(truthyValue, 'This should not throw!');
invariant(falsyValue, 'This will throw!');

warning(truthyValue, 'This should not log a warning');
warning(falsyValue, 'This should log a warning');

if ( __DEV__ ) {
  // this is excluded from the production bundle
}

### Tests

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#tests)
Tests are setup using [vitest](https://npmjs.com/vitest). They can be run once with coverage with `yarn test` or use `yarn test:watch` to run them in watch mode.

```
yarn test:watch
```

### Pull Requests

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#pull-requests)
When contributing changes, make sure to document them in a changeset. You'll usually do this when you're ready to push the changes and create a pull-request. To do so, run:

```
yarn changeset
```

`Changeset` will ask you for a change description, and how different packages are affected. Your answers help us determine if packages should be bumped in version, and if it should be a `patch`, `minor`, or `major` version bump. The description you provide may end up in our changelogs, please write it in a way that makes sense to our users.

Please review, polish, and commit the files after completing the steps.

Please use a descriptive title for pull-request. The title should be in semantic commit format, so we can use it as (squash merge) commit message and automate the release process.

Please add a description that explains the change. The changeset is likely to form a solid basis for your description, tho the pull-request description is written for our maintainers and thereby may be more technical and explain why certain decisions were made.

## Publish

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#publish)
Publishing new package versions is an automated process managed via the `release` workflow. To trigger a release, push a commit containing changesets (`yarn changeset`) to `main`. This will trigger the bot to open or update a pull-request named `next release`. Once that pull request gets merged, the bot will publish the new versions to npm and create the release notes on GitHub.

### Manual publishing

[](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js#manual-publishing)
Manual publishing is done in two steps:

```
yarn changeset:version
```

This command consumes the changesets as collected in [.changeset](https://github.com/magicbell/magicbell-js/blob/main/.changeset), and proposes changelogs and version bumps. Please review the proposed changes, and polish the changelogs. Make sure that breaking changes result in major version bumps.

Commit the change with `git add . && git commit -m 'version packages'`, and move on to the next step to publish.

```
yarn changeset:release
```

This will publish all changed packages to npm, and tag the last commit. Please don't commit anything between the release commit, and the publish action. These command are separate to enable you to check if the release commit is accurate.

part.text: The GitHub readme is for the magicbell-js monorepo, but that's the client-side SDK. The project I need to work with uses `magicbell-js/project-client` which is a different package. Let me look at the workflows service source code and the workflow definition models to understand how to use the `saveWorkflow` method.
Let me examine the key source files to understand the SDK's workflow API structure.
Now I have a clear picture of the SDK. Let me also look at the `SdkConfig` type to understand how to initialize the client, and the package.json to understand the module structure.

Now I have all the information I need to create the script. Let me summarize what I've found:

1. The project uses `"type": "commonjs"` in package.json, so I should use `require()` syntax
2. The SDK is imported from `magicbell-js/project-client`
3. The `Client` class from `magicbell-js/project-client` has a `workflows` property with a `saveWorkflow` method
4. The `saveWorkflow` method takes a `WorkflowDefinition` object as its body parameter
5. The `WorkflowDefinition` has:
   - `key`: string (min 3 chars, regex `/^[A-Za-z0-9\_\.\\-\\:]+$/`)
   - `steps`: array of `WorkflowDefinitionSteps`
   - `disabled`: optional boolean
6. Each `WorkflowDefinitionSteps` has:
   - `command`: string (regex `/^[a-z_]+$/`) - e.g., "broadcast", "wait", etc.
   - `if`: optional string
   - `input`: optional any
7. The `SdkConfig` takes `token` for authentication
8. The `saveWorkflow` method returns `Promise<HttpResponse<WorkflowDefinition>>`

Now, for the workflow steps:
1. `broadcast` command with title "Welcome!" - the `input` should contain the notification details
2. `wait` command with `duration` of 60 seconds - the `input` should have `{ duration: 60 }`
3. `broadcast` command with title "Follow up" - the `input` should contain the notification details

The project is set up as CommonJS, so I'll use `require()`.

Let me also check the `HttpResponse` type to understand what the response looks like.

The key format must be `workflow-delay-${run-id}` where `run-id` comes from `ZEALT_RUN_ID` env var.

The client is initialized with `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN` env var as the `token` in `SdkConfig`.

Let me create the script now.

3. Use `workflows.saveWorkflow` to create a workflow with:
   - key: `workflow-delay-${runId}`
   - steps: 
     - broadcast with title "Welcome!"
     - wait with duration 60
     - broadcast with title "Follow up"
4. Print a success message

input.result:
Created `/home/user/magicbell-task/create-workflow.js` which uses the MagicBell Node.js SDK to define and save a workflow with a delay step.

The script:
1. Reads `ZEALT_RUN_ID` from the environment to construct the workflow key as `workflow-delay-${runId}`
2. Initializes the `ProjectClient` from `magicbell-js/project-client` using `MAGICBELL_PROJECT_TOKEN` from the environment
3. Calls `client.workflows.saveWorkflow()` with a workflow definition containing three steps in order:
   - `broadcast` command with title "Welcome!"
   - `wait` command with `duration: 60` (seconds)
   - `broadcast` command with title "Follow up"
4. Prints a success message upon completion: `Workflow "workflow-delay-{runId}" created successfully!`

Also installed the missing `tslib` dependency required by the SDK.
